chunk_summary: The appetite test is a critical triage tool for determining outpatient eligibility in SAM management, assessing metabolic stability and readiness for therapeutic feeding at home versus inpatient care for complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of the appetite test in triaging SAM patients for outpatient care.
Learn the practical steps for conducting the appetite test effectively and objectively.
Identify clinical signs of test failure and their implications for inpatient stabilization.
Recognize the importance of re-testing only after stabilization in cases of suspected hypoglycemia, hypothermia, or sepsis.
key_facts: The appetite test assesses readiness for outpatient therapeutic feeding using RUTF or locally prepared therapeutic feeds.
Failure criteria include inability to consume the minimum program amount for weight band, repeated vomiting, lethargy, or respiratory distress during feeding.
A 'fail' triggers inpatient stabilization actions such as warmth, glucose prevention, and empiric antibiotics for complications.
Re-testing should only occur after clinical improvement to avoid misclassification of severity.
Conduct the test in a calm area, ensuring the child has not had a substantial feed for about 2 hours prior.
reference_quote: Use the appetite test as a triage gate for outpatient eligibility and as an early marker of metabolic stability in SAM. (Source: https://nhm.gov.in/images/pdf/programmes/child-health/IEC)
document_key_topics: Appetite test protocol for SAM outpatient eligibility
Criteria for inpatient stabilization in SAM management
Practical execution and interpretation of appetite tests
